text: The surgeon performs a RTAP ventral incisional hernia repair above the umbilicus, using three 8mm trocars on the patient's left side. The surgeon begins developing a flap ipsilateral to the camera from the median fat pad, carefully dissecting the thin peritoneum with blunt dissection and focused cautery. The flap is developed with a larger pocket on the contralateral side to reduce tension when covering the mesh.

The surgeon uses a heavyweight proline mesh, measuring fifteen by fifteen centimeters, to address the hernia in a patient with a higher BMI. The dissection proceeds bluntly across the median fat pad and over the falciform ligament, demonstrating the medial ligaments and teres. The surgeon carefully dissects above and below the hernia defect, creating a 'volcano sign' to facilitate the reduction of hernia contents. Visible scarring from previous incisions is noted in the hypogastrium as the surgeon reduces the multi-lobed hernia defect.

The surgeon begins dissection on the patient's right-hand side, encountering a thin peritoneum near the lateral aspect of the fat pad. Using blunt dissection, the surgeon carefully navigates into the proper plane, avoiding the creation of holes. Despite a superior attempt resulting in a hole, the inferior approach allows for smooth separation of tissue layers, maintaining the integrity of the transversalis fascia.

The surgeon follows the dissection plane laterally and superiorly, closing small holes in the peritoneum with 3-0 Vicryl sutures. A large pocket is created for mesh placement, with the flap dissected as far laterally as possible to reduce tension. The dissection continues above the falciform ligament, with visible blunt dissection posterior to a previously created hole.

The surgeon meticulously maintains hemostasis while performing inferior dissection below the arcuate line, revealing the bare muscle belly of the rectus abdominis. The dissection creates sufficient space for a 15x15 mesh. The surgeon reduces pressure to about eight and begins defect closure, noting the bilobed nature of the hernia defect, which appears to come together vertically.

The surgeon places a 15x15 mesh into the hernia defect, anchoring it at the midline with a trio silk suture. The mesh is secured with interrupted sutures at all four corners. The ipsilateral side of the mesh extends outside the pocket due to the initial lateral dissection, but the flap's laxity accommodates this placement.

The surgeon uses Vicryl sutures to close holes in the abdominal wall before closing the flap over the mesh. The flap is sewn from right to left, extending beyond the edge of the mesh to prevent folding. Care is taken to avoid grabbing the epigastric vessels during the suturing process.
